Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Lisa Feldman Barrett

b. 1963

Lisa Feldman Barrett is not, in the first instance, writing a classical treatise on the soul.

Lisa Feldman Barrett on mind, consciousness, soul, and theological anthropology

Lisa Feldman Barrett is not, in the first instance, writing a classical treatise on the soul. She is a psychologist and neuroscientist whose theory of constructed emotion has gradually become a broader theory of mind. That broader ambition is now explicit: in 2024 she presented “Relational Realism as a Framework for Studying the Mind” in the Rudolf Carnap Lectures and “Constructing the Mind” and “Relational Realism” in the Pufendorf Lectures; Northeastern’s profile of those lectures describes her work as a general framework for how a brain, in conversation with body and world, creates mind.

The deepest reconstruction is this: Barrett’s philosophy of mind is an anti-Cartesian, anti-essentialist, embodied, predictive, allostatic, socially scaffolded, relational realism. The mind is not a private inner substance added to a body. Nor is it a set of fixed “faculties” such as reason, emotion, will, and perception, each with its own natural essence. Mind is what a living organism does as a brain regulates a body in a world with other bodies, using learned concepts to predict, act, feel, perceive, remember, and become a self. Consciousness is not a neutral theater onto which emotions are later projected; it is already affectively textured by the body’s ongoing regulatory condition. The “soul,” if retained within a Barrett-compatible theology, cannot be a detachable ghostly organ of cognition. It would have to be reconceived as embodied, relational, socially formed personhood: the living form of a human being as she is patterned by body, world, language, practices, memory, love, and responsibility.

That last sentence is my reconstruction, not Barrett’s own theological doctrine. Barrett’s own texts mainly treat “soul” historically: as part of the inherited intellectual machinery that separated body from mind, emotion from reason, and scientific explanation from theological interiority. Her work does not prove that theological claims about the soul are false. It does, however, make certain popular soul-concepts scientifically and philosophically difficult: especially the idea that the real person is an inner, disembodied rational self that merely uses the body and is occasionally disrupted by emotion.

1. The target: faculty psychology, essentialism, and the inherited “inner realm”

Barrett’s project begins by rejecting a very old map of the mind. She argues that Western thought has repeatedly divided the mind into faculties such as cognition, emotion, volition, and perception, and then treated those faculties as if they were natural divisions in the brain. In her 2017 theory article, she traces this habit to ancient philosophy and medicine, noting that modern emotion science often began with everyday emotion words and then searched for neural essences corresponding to them.

This is not a small technical disagreement about emotion labels. It is a critique of a metaphysics of mind. Barrett is attacking what philosophers would call psychological essentialism: the assumption that anger, fear, rationality, selfhood, memory, and other mental kinds each possess an inner essence, often imagined as a dedicated brain region, circuit, module, or faculty. Her official page for How Emotions Are Made states the thesis in popular form: emotions do not live in distinct brain parts, are not universally expressed and recognized in a fixed way, and are constructed in the moment by core systems interacting across the whole brain, aided by lifetime learning.

Her later work sharpens this anti-essentialism. In a 2025 article defending the theory of constructed emotion against attempts to reconcile it with basic emotion theory, Barrett and colleagues insist that the two frameworks are not merely rival lists of findings but incommensurable theoretical approaches. Basic emotion theory, in their view, treats emotion categories typologically, as if each emotion type has a prototype or essence; constructed emotion theory uses “population thinking,” where variation is not noise around an ideal form but part of what the category is.

That move matters for philosophy of mind because it generalizes. A mind is not a hierarchy of fixed inner powers. It is a dynamic system of context-sensitive, functional, relational categorizations. Barrett’s 2025 article describes categories in the theory of constructed emotion as functional, relational, situated, and ad hoc; a category is something the organism does in a concrete situation, not something the organism simply has as a stored essence.

Her critique of soul-language belongs here. In Seven and a Half Lessons About the Brain, she treats old images of the brain as a “house for the soul,” as well as phrenology, left-brain/right-brain stories, and System 1/System 2 contrasts, as metaphors that can mislead when taken as literal maps of mental architecture. Barrett’s critique is not merely that old theology used bad anatomy. It is that the very desire to locate inner faculties in brain places, chambers, or modules is a recurring mistake.

2. The positive architecture: allostasis, prediction, interoception, concepts

Barrett’s alternative begins with the organism rather than introspective categories. She argues that the brain’s central biological task is not “thinking” in the abstract, but regulating the body so that the organism can survive and act. In her public scientific writing and interviews, this is often called managing the body budget; in technical terms, it is allostasis, the anticipatory regulation of bodily systems.

In her 2017 theory article, allostasis is defined as regulating the internal milieu by anticipating physiological needs before they arise, and concepts are described as embodied, whole-brain representations that predict what will happen, what action is best, and what the allostatic consequences will be. That definition gives the skeleton of her philosophy of mind. A concept is not merely a word or a mental label; it is an embodied predictive skill for acting in the world.

The brain, in this picture, is a predictive organ. It does not passively wait for stimuli and then react. It uses an internal model to anticipate sensory inputs from the world and the body, prepare action, and correct prediction error when the world resists. In Barrett’s 2017 formulation, simulations guide action and construct perception; the brain prepares competing simulations, categorizes incoming signals, and perception is dependent on action rather than being a stimulus-response process.

This is why the body is not an external object to the mind. Barrett’s work on interoception and allostasis argues that bodily regulation and bodily sensation are constitutive of ordinary mental life. A 2017 Nature Human Behaviour article by Barrett and Simmons describes an allostatic–interoceptive system and says the findings have implications for dissolving the mind/body boundary and for unifying mental and physical illness. A Northeastern summary of that work says the research breaks down the barrier between mind and body and that feelings are part of any mental event, not an optional emotional add-on.

The philosophical consequence is strong: mind is not brain alone, and certainly not “rational brain” alone. Mind is brain–body regulation under predictive control. It is not reducible to mere conscious thought, because much of the predictive regulation is nonconscious. It is not reducible to brute physiology either, because bodily signals are interpreted through concepts, history, culture, language, and social reality.

3. Emotion as constructed, real, and non-illusory

Barrett’s theory of emotion is the clearest entry point into her philosophy of mind. Her core claim is often misunderstood as “emotions are fake.” That is not her view. Her view is that emotions are not biologically pre-packaged essences such as a fear circuit, anger circuit, or sadness module. They are real events constructed by a brain using interoception, concepts, predictions, action tendencies, and social learning.

In the 2017 theory article, she says emotion experience and emotion perception arise when past emotion experience is used to categorize a predicted sensory array; the same brain processes that construct perception also construct emotion. Later in the article she summarizes the theory this way: the brain runs an internal model to control bodily pattern generators in the service of allostasis; that model uses concepts built from past experience; when prediction error is sufficiently minimized, prediction becomes perception or experience, with affect made available to consciousness.

The distinction between affect and emotion is decisive. Affect is the ongoing, low-dimensional feeling tone of bodily life: pleasantness or unpleasantness, agitation or calm, energy or depletion. Emotion is what happens when affective bodily significance is categorized with concepts such as anger, grief, shame, joy, reverence, contrition, or gratitude. Barrett explicitly identifies allostasis, interoception, affect, and concepts as species-general ingredients, while emotion concepts are more species-specific and culturally learned; confusing those levels is a category error.

That distinction also helps with the theological dimension. Affective life is not a fallen, irrational interruption of the mind. It is one of the primitive ways the organism’s condition becomes meaningful. Emotion, meanwhile, is conceptually and socially shaped. A person does not merely “have” anger as a natural eruption; anger is an event in which body, learned categories, context, attention, action, and social norms converge.

Barrett’s realism about constructed emotion is close to her realism about social facts. She compares emotions to money: socially made, yet real. In the 2017 theory article she explicitly denies that emotions are illusions and says emotion categories lack dedicated neural essences while still being real in the way money is real. Her official social-reality essays make the same point more generally: money and countries are “made up” in one sense, but they are real because human brains coordinate around them, and social reality can shape physical health and even biological development.

This gives Barrett a distinctive ontology: constructed does not mean arbitrary; socially real does not mean unreal; bodily grounded does not mean biologically fixed. Emotion is neither a natural essence waiting inside the organism nor a mere linguistic fiction. It is a real, situated, embodied, socially intelligible event.

4. Consciousness: affective, embodied, and action-oriented

Barrett does not offer a full metaphysical solution to the hard problem of consciousness. Her work does not explain, in Chalmers-style terms, why physical processes should be accompanied by subjective experience at all. What she does provide is a rich theory of the structure and contents of lived experience: how a brain–body system makes a world meaningful, affective, and actionable.

Her clearest claim about consciousness is that affect is a property of consciousness. In an endnote to How Emotions Are Made, she says the brain’s representation of interoceptive change produces feelings such as pleasantness, unpleasantness, agitation, and calmness, and that affect accompanies us from birth to death in a way analogous to perceptual properties such as brightness and loudness.

This has several philosophical implications.

First, consciousness is never purely “cognitive.” Every conscious state has bodily significance. Even seeing a chair, remembering a person, judging an argument, or hearing a doctrine occurs with some degree of ease, tension, interest, fatigue, attraction, aversion, calm, or agitation. The mind is always already valuing the world in relation to bodily regulation.

Second, conscious experience is not a mirror of the world. Barrett often describes everyday experience as a controlled hallucination constrained by the world and the body. That language should not be read as skepticism about reality; it means perception is actively constructed by prediction and corrected by constraint.

Third, consciousness is action-oriented. The brain does not first build a neutral representation and then decide what to do. It predicts in order to act, and perception is part of action-guidance. Barrett’s 2017 paper explicitly rejects the stimulus-response model and treats perception as dependent on action under active inference.

Fourth, consciousness is not exhausted by private interiority. Because concepts are culturally learned and social reality shapes perception, one’s stream of consciousness is already socially scaffolded. What a person can feel, notice, name, tolerate, morally evaluate, and spiritually interpret depends partly on the concepts available in that person’s community.

In this framework, consciousness is not the soul’s private chamber. It is the organism’s lived world as disclosed through prediction, body regulation, concept use, and social participation.

5. Selfhood: no inner homunculus, no isolated self

Barrett’s relational realism deepens the theory of self. A Northeastern account of her 2024 lectures reports her claim that there is no brain place dedicated to anger, rationality, or the self, and describes the brain as being in constant conversation with the body and with other brains-in-bodies.

The self, therefore, is not a hidden spectator behind experience. It is a relational construction: an ongoing pattern by which a living body is regulated, remembered, narrated, addressed, and socially recognized. The same Northeastern article quotes Hazel Markus’s line, used in Barrett’s context, that one cannot be a self by oneself.

This is not the annihilation of selfhood. It is a rejection of the isolated Cartesian ego. A person is real, but personhood is relationally constituted. One becomes a self through body regulation, language, attachment, social norms, memory, expectation, and mutual recognition. Barrett’s social-reality writings extend this: our shared concepts and institutions do not float above biology; they shape bodily life, health, action, and future prediction.

The theological significance is immediate. A Barrett-compatible anthropology will not treat the soul as an inner atom of selfhood, complete before embodiment and community. It will treat the human person as intrinsically embodied and intrinsically relational. That is far closer to covenantal, liturgical, and communal views of personhood than to a modern picture of the soul as a private mental container.

6. Relational realism: between naive realism and subjectivism

Barrett’s philosophical name for the broader framework is relational realism. The phrase is important because it prevents two bad readings. She is not a naive realist who thinks the mind simply copies a ready-made world. She is also not a subjectivist who thinks reality is whatever we feel or say it is.

In the Northeastern profile of her 2024 lectures, Barrett contrasts traditional realism with a relational account: color, for example, is not simply a property sitting in objects independently of perceivers; it is a relation among light, surfaces, bodies, nervous systems, and perceiving organisms. The article summarizes relational realism as the view that what is real exists in relation to something else, often the organism.

Her social-reality essays add a necessary constraint. Social reality is powerful, but physical reality constrains it. We can collectively create money, borders, reputations, professions, rituals, legal categories, and social identities, but we cannot narrate away biological, ecological, or physical limits without consequences.

This is one of the most promising parts of Barrett’s thought for theology. It gives a way to say that meanings, practices, offices, vocations, rituals, vows, sins, and reconciliations can be real without being reducible to physical essences. A marriage, a confession, a blessing, a stigma, a nation, a trauma narrative, or a liturgical identity may be socially constructed and still be real. But it also insists that social reality must answer to bodies, suffering, material constraints, and the wider world.

7. Barrett’s explicit comments on soul and Christian history

Barrett’s most direct discussions of “soul” appear in historical notes rather than constructive theology. In an endnote to How Emotions Are Made, she says medieval Christian theologians inherited and transformed ancient Greek ideas of psyche, and she presents a historical trajectory in which emotions were increasingly placed in the body while cognition came to be associated with the immortal soul. She also discusses the medieval localization of faculties such as memory, imagination, and intelligence in brain ventricles.

In the continuation of that note, she discusses Augustine, Aquinas, Nemesius, Renaissance and Enlightenment developments, and Descartes. Her narrative emphasizes the eventual Cartesian separation of mind from anatomical body, with the mind treated as an ineffable soul beyond scientific investigation. She also notes that some historians interpret Descartes’s framing as partly strategic in relation to church authority and scientific survival.

These comments should not be dismissed as incidental. They show what Barrett thinks her science is undoing: the long-standing division between bodily emotion and rational soul, between lower affect and higher cognition, between fleshly disturbance and mental purity.

Still, her historical account needs theological care. Christian anthropology is not identical with Cartesian dualism. Aquinas, for example, is a much more complex case: the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that Aquinas treats the human being in this life as the whole composite of form and matter, even though he also argues for an incorporeal, subsistent human soul and eventual reunion with the resurrected body. The Stanford article on dualism similarly notes that hylomorphism identifies soul with the form of a living creature and need not entail ordinary mind-body dualism, even though Aquinas’s version introduces further complexities about immaterial intellect.

So the rigorous theological reading is this: Barrett’s work is devastating for crude Cartesian, faculty-based, anti-body interpretations of the soul. It is not automatically devastating for all Christian doctrines of soul, especially hylomorphic, incarnational, sacramental, or holistic accounts. It forces theology to ask which “soul” it means.

8. A Barrett-compatible account of the soul

Barrett herself does not need the soul as an explanatory entity. Her model explains emotion, perception, affect, selfhood, and much of conscious life through brain–body–world prediction, allostasis, concept learning, and social reality. In that sense, her science is methodologically naturalistic.

But theology is not obligated to use “soul” as a rival neurological mechanism. If soul is defined as a hidden mental organ that produces rational thought apart from the body, Barrett’s framework strongly resists it. If soul is defined as the form, life, and morally accountable pattern of the embodied person, Barrett’s framework can be appropriated more fruitfully.

A Barrett-compatible theological soul would have at least six features.

It would be embodied. The person’s interior life is not separable from metabolic regulation, sleep, stress, posture, illness, hunger, touch, pain, and hormonal life. Barrett’s allostatic model makes bodily regulation central to thought, emotion, decision-making, and selfhood.

It would be affective. A soul is not pure intellect. It is a living orientation toward the world, always involving attraction, aversion, calm, threat, desire, fatigue, receptivity, and resistance. Barrett’s claim that affect is a property of consciousness supports a theological anthropology in which affect is fundamental rather than spiritually embarrassing.

It would be conceptual. Human beings experience the world through learned categories. A soul, in this reconstructed sense, has a grammar of feeling and action: it has learned what counts as shame, honor, reverence, insult, mercy, temptation, peace, holiness, disgust, repentance, or joy.

It would be social. Because emotion concepts and social realities are collectively sustained, the soul is not formed alone. Families, churches, nations, schools, media, rituals, technologies, and economies help build the concepts through which bodies interpret themselves.

It would be historical. Past experience becomes the predictive basis for present perception and future action. Barrett’s interview comments emphasize that past experience helps create the present, while changing present experience can seed different future predictions.

It would be normatively open. Barrett can describe how concepts form and how bodies regulate, but she does not by herself supply the theological telos of the person. Theology would have to say what the person is for: love, communion with God, holiness, justice, beatitude, resurrection, or some other final end.

This means Barrett can supply a powerful mechanism of formation, but not a complete doctrine of salvation, sanctification, or beatitude.

9. Theological anthropology: reason, emotion, desire, and formation

The strongest theological implication of Barrett’s work is that the old opposition between reason and emotion becomes untenable. Barrett’s mind/body research explicitly argues that feelings are part of mental events and that the division between rational and irrational processes is artificial.

Some theological scholarship is already moving in this direction. A 2023 article on Christian anthropology argues that reason, emotion, and desire should be understood as united and Godward, and that their well-ordered collaboration reflects divine oneness and holiness. That claim is not Barrett’s claim, but Barrett’s work gives it neurocognitive support against a simplistic “reason good, emotion dangerous” model.

Another article in the Journal of Spiritual Formation and Soul Care argues that modern discourse moved from the older language of passions and affections of the soul toward “emotions of the body,” excluding theology and losing Judeo-Christian knowledge concerning soul, will, and love. Barrett’s own historical comments are compatible with that diagnosis, though from a different angle: she sees the separation of body, soul, cognition, and emotion as part of the conceptual inheritance that science must overcome.

Biblical and liturgical formation become especially important. A theological essay on the Psalms and Barrett’s conceptual-act theory argues that emotions are not evolutionary leftovers but are deeply involved in theological anthropology; the Psalms can function as learned affective scripts that refine emotion concepts and open experience to God. This is one of the most fruitful theological uses of Barrett: prayer, lament, confession, praise, fasting, song, Eucharist, kneeling, silence, pilgrimage, and communal worship are not merely expressions of an already-formed inner soul. They are practices by which embodied persons acquire new concepts, regulate affect, re-pattern attention, and inhabit a different social reality.

The theological payoff is significant. Spiritual formation is not simply the communication of propositions to a rational soul. It is the formation of predictive life: what the person expects, notices, fears, loves, names, remembers, and can bear. A Barrett-informed theology of formation would treat doctrines, rituals, and communities as concept-shaping and body-shaping practices.

10. Sin, grace, and social reality

Barrett does not offer a doctrine of sin. But her account of social reality provides a powerful tool for describing how sin can become embodied and institutional. Her social-reality essays say that human beings create powerful shared realities through storytelling and coordination, and that social realities such as race stereotypes can become physical realities in brain wiring and health.

A theological interpretation can take that seriously without reducing sin to neuroscience. Sin is not merely bad information in private minds. It can be a social world that trains bodies to perceive some people as threats, others as inferior, some pleasures as necessary, some injustices as normal, and some cruelties as righteous. In Barrett’s terms, a sinful social reality supplies concepts and predictions that organize perception and action before reflective deliberation begins.

Grace, likewise, need not be imagined only as a private invisible event in an inner soul. A Barrett-informed account would also look for grace in re-formed social reality: communities of forgiveness, practices of attention, bodily safety, truthful naming, shared lament, and new concepts for seeing oneself and others. Theology would add what Barrett’s science does not: that such re-formation may be participation in divine action, not merely psychological adaptation.

This also means pastoral care cannot be reduced to “think differently.” Since allostasis matters, spiritual life is affected by sleep, food, chronic stress, trauma, illness, touch, poverty, loneliness, and threat. Barrett’s work does not secularize spiritual care; it makes bad spiritual care harder to defend. A theology that ignores the body is ignoring part of the mind.

11. Freedom, responsibility, and moral agency

Barrett’s theory changes the shape of free will. It does not picture freedom as a little sovereign chooser standing outside causality and selecting emotions in the moment. Nor does it collapse responsibility into determinism.

Her interview remarks offer a more prospective model: past experiences help create present perception and action, but changing present experience can seed different predictions for the future. She emphasizes cultivating new experiences, changing environments, and broadening concepts as ways of increasing future flexibility.

Moral agency, then, is not primarily the power to command an emotion at the instant it appears. It is the longer-term power, personal and communal, to shape the conditions under which future emotions, perceptions, and actions become likely. Responsibility becomes developmental and ecological. One is responsible not only for isolated choices but for habits, practices, media diets, relationships, institutions, and bodily conditions that train prediction.

This is the point at which Barrett’s thought converges with virtue ethics and spiritual discipline. Virtue is not just an intellectual commitment to goodness. It is the acquired capacity to perceive, feel, and act rightly because one’s body, concepts, and social world have been trained over time. Aquinas’s account of virtue as stable, prompt, pleasurable action has a structural kinship here, even though Aquinas and Barrett have very different metaphysical commitments. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that Aquinas treats virtue as necessary for reliable moral activity over time, including promptness and taking pleasure in the action as a kind of second nature.

12. International reception and non-English emphases

The non-English reception I found largely confirms the main Anglo-American interpretation, but it sometimes foregrounds different stakes: mind–body integration, legal responsibility, clinical use, AI ethics, and spiritual or cultural context.

French reception is especially useful on legal and social implications. A review in the Revue québécoise de psychologie presents Barrett’s view as rejecting emotion fingerprints, universality, and context-free recognition, and it emphasizes that emotions are constructed from physical properties and learned concepts by a flexible brain. It also highlights the legal implications: the “reasonable person,” remorse, extreme emotion defenses, and courtroom judgments all rely on questionable assumptions about emotion and rationality. A French article in La Vie, a publication with explicit Christian coverage, frames constructed emotion theory as putting bodily sensation at the center of emotional dynamics and links moods to bodily perception.

Spanish-language sources often foreground interoception and the collapse of the mind/body split. One Spanish neuroscience article summarizes Barrett’s theory as the view that emotions are actively constructed by the brain to make sense of internal and external sensations in context, while also describing affect or core affect as continuous bodily feeling along valence and arousal dimensions. A Spanish piece from Nirakara emphasizes that there is no sadness or anger center, that each emotion is formed from past experience, environment, and interoception, and that the separation of physical and mental health is a cultural construction rather than a deep biological truth.

German reception is often precise about the affect/emotion distinction. A German discussion in managerSeminare explains that affective states are not emotions; they are summaries of bodily interiority such as well-being, malaise, arousal, and calm, while emotions are hypotheses that interpret bodily signals and external information through simulations and prior concepts. That is a concise philosophical point: Barrett is not saying “everything is emotion”; she is saying affective consciousness is basic, while named emotions are constructed categorizations.

Portuguese-language clinical reception likewise stresses construction, prediction, interoception, and culture. A Portuguese medical/psychiatry article summarizes Barrett’s view as rejecting universal automatic reactions and treating emotions as constructions based on past experience, cultural context, bodily interpretations, interoception, context, and exteroception. A Portuguese neuroscience/clinical piece applies the theory to psychotherapy and education, emphasizing that emotions integrate body, context, and learning rather than mapping rigidly onto fixed physiological patterns.

Dutch reception extends Barrett into social and ecological contexts. A Dutch article by Jaap van der Stel uses Barrett’s theory in relation to climate emotions, stressing that emotions are constructed by the brain and shaped by language, context, and social interaction. French-language material on the theory’s AI implications also raises cultural, religious, sociopolitical, and ethical concerns about emotion recognition and affective computing, precisely because Barrett’s theory makes emotion contextual rather than universally readable from faces or physiology.

The most interesting non-English critique I found comes from Italian reception. An Italian critical discussion worries that Barrett’s theory may overcorrect against universalist emotion theories, may be too Western in some assumptions, may lack operational predictivity, and may risk reducing emotions to cognitive constructions unless integrated with theorists such as Panksepp, Ekman, Scherer, Izard, and Plutchik. That criticism is worth taking seriously. It does not refute Barrett, but it warns against turning “constructed” into “merely cognitive” or “merely linguistic.”

13. Main objections and limits

The first limit is that Barrett’s theory is not universally accepted in affective neuroscience. Ralph Adolphs, for example, defends the view that emotions are biological functional states and distinguishes emotion states from conscious experiences and concepts. He argues for cross-species work, lesion studies, and ecological neuroscience, and he resists making human emotion concepts the center of the phenomenon.

This matters philosophically because animals, infants, and prelinguistic human beings seem to have affective and emotion-like life before they possess rich language. Barrett can respond that allostasis, interoception, affect, and action are species-general ingredients, while human emotion concepts are more culturally elaborated. But critics will continue to ask whether her theory underestimates evolved affective organization.

The second limit is the hard problem of consciousness. Barrett gives a theory of how experience is structured, predicted, categorized, and affectively textured. She does not demonstrate why such processes should be accompanied by phenomenal subjectivity at all. Her theory is therefore more powerful as a theory of conscious contents, affective meaning, and mental construction than as a final metaphysics of consciousness.

The third limit is theological. Barrett’s historical remarks about Christian thought are useful but compressed. They correctly identify a real lineage of body/soul, reason/emotion, and cognition/affect splits, but Christian anthropology includes more than that lineage: biblical holism, patristic complexity, Thomistic hylomorphism, sacramental embodiment, resurrection theology, and contemporary non-Cartesian accounts of personhood all complicate the picture.

The fourth limit is ethical. If emotions are constructed, some readers hear “you are responsible for every emotion you have.” That is too simplistic. Barrett’s theory emphasizes plasticity and responsibility, but also shows that prediction is shaped by past experience, body budget, stress, trauma, social environment, and collective reality. Responsibility exists, but it is distributed across persons, practices, institutions, and histories.

14. The extracted philosophy of mind: twelve theses

Barrett’s implicit philosophy of mind can be stated as twelve theses.

First, anti-essentialism: mental categories are not guaranteed to have hidden natural essences simply because folk psychology names them. Anger, fear, rationality, selfhood, and consciousness should not be presumed to correspond to fixed brain organs or modules.

Second, allostatic primacy: the brain’s central task is anticipatory regulation of the body. Thought, perception, emotion, attention, and action are organized around the organism’s ongoing need to manage energy, threat, opportunity, and bodily viability.

Third, predictive construction: perception and emotion are not passive reactions to stimuli. They are active predictions constrained by sensory input, bodily condition, and the world.

Fourth, embodied conceptualism: concepts are not merely abstract labels. They are embodied, action-guiding predictions that allow the organism to make sense of sensation and choose what to do.

Fifth, affective consciousness: consciousness is always affectively toned. Affect is not a special emotion but a basic property of conscious life arising from interoceptive regulation.

Sixth, constructed emotion realism: emotions are constructed, but real. They are not illusions, and they are not arbitrary inventions. They are real events produced through body, concept, action, and social coordination.

Seventh, social ontology: human mental life is partly constituted by social reality. Money, nations, legal identities, emotion concepts, gender norms, racial stereotypes, religious roles, and moral categories can shape bodies and brains because people collectively enact them.

Eighth, relational realism: reality is not merely mind-independent essence or subjective projection. Many real properties exist relationally, through organism–world–body–culture interactions.

Ninth, non-Cartesian selfhood: the self is not an inner spectator or soul-substance localized in the brain. It is a relational, embodied, socially formed pattern of regulation, memory, concept use, and recognition.

Tenth, formative freedom: agency is less a momentary power to command inner states than a long-term capacity to reshape the conditions under which future perception, emotion, and action are predicted.

Eleventh, theological compatibility with embodied soul: Barrett’s framework is incompatible with a crude ghost-in-the-machine soul, but compatible with a theology that treats soul as embodied, relational, affective, and socially formed personhood. That compatibility is a theological reconstruction, not an explicit Barrett doctrine.

Twelfth, need for telos: Barrett’s science explains mechanisms of formation; theology must supply final ends. Neuroscience can describe how people learn reverence, shame, peace, anger, or compassion. It cannot by itself say whether the proper end of the human person is communion with God, justice, flourishing, sanctification, or beatitude.

Final synthesis

Barrett’s work gives us a philosophy of mind in which the mind is not a thing hidden inside the head, consciousness is not a disembodied light, emotion is not an irrational animal force, and the soul is not a scientific posit. The mind is an embodied, predictive, relational process by which a living organism regulates itself and makes a world meaningful. Consciousness is the lived surface of that process, saturated by affect. Emotion is a real but constructed achievement of body, concept, action, and social life. Selfhood is relational. Freedom is formative. Social reality is powerful but constrained.

Theologically, Barrett should not be read as a casual eliminator of the soul. She is more interesting than that. Her work forces theology to abandon weak dualisms and recover stronger accounts of embodied personhood. It makes the Psalms, liturgy, virtue, spiritual discipline, community, trauma, social sin, and bodily care newly central to the doctrine of the person. It also leaves theology with real work to do: to articulate what “soul” means if it is not a detachable inner substance, and to state the telos of the embodied mind beyond what neuroscience can provide.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
